Title: Thunderstorms expected in northern states until noon
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian Meteorological Department (MetMalaysia) has issued a thunderstorm warning involving heavy rain and strong winds for four states until 12pm today.
The states involved are Perlis, Kedah, Perak and Penang.
The affected areas are Langkawi, Kubang Pasu, Kota Setar, Yan and Kuala Muda in Kedah.
A similar warning was also issued for Kerian in Perak and the entire state of Penang.
"Thunderstorm warnings are short-term warnings valid for not more than six hours for each issuance," it said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ringgit expected to stay softer vs US dollar next week
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The ringgit is expected to trade on a softer note against the US dollar next week amid persistent expectations of a possible US Federal Reserve (Fed) rate hike later this year.
Bank Muamalat Malaysia Bhd chief economist Dr Mohd Afzanizam Abdul Rashid said expectations of a possible 25-basis-point increase in the federal fund rate later this year are likely to continue supporting the greenback and weigh on regional currencies, including the ringgit.
"Next week, we foresee similar dynamics will continue to hold," he told Bernama.
Mohd Afzanizam said investors will closely monitor the release of the United States Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) Price Index on June 26, which is the Fed's preferred measure of inflation.
"The inflation gauge is expected to remain elevated. As such, expect the ringgit to stay soft in the near term," he said.
On a Friday-to-Friday basis, the ringgit eased to 4.1340/1395 against the US dollar from 4.0555/0600 a week earlier.
The local currency traded lower against a basket of major currencies during the week.
It depreciated against the British pound to 5.4709/4782 from 5.4429/4489, eased versus the Japanese yen to 2.5636/5671 from 2.5334/5364 and weakened against the euro to 4.7376/7439 from 4.6979/7031 previously.
It also traded lower against Asean currencies.
It slid against the Indonesian rupiah to 232.2/232.5 from 227.0/227.4, eased vis-a-vis the Singapore dollar to 3.2019/2064 from 3.1602/1640, slipped against the Thai baht to 12.5856/6074 from 12.4105/4288, and declined against the Philippine peso to 6.80/6.81 from 6.67/6.68.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bursa Malaysia seen trading range-bound next week amid geopolitical uncertainties
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Bursa Malaysia is expected to trade in a range-bound mode next week as investors continue to monitor developments in the US-Iran nuclear negotiations and assess the sustainability of the recently announced peace agreement.
IPPFA Sdn Bhd director of investment strategy and country economist Mohd Sedek Jantan said that while geopolitical risks have moderated, uncertainty remains over the implementation of the deal and the pace of normalisation in the Strait of Hormuz.
On the positive side, he said the interim peace agreement signed last week and the partial resumption of oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz have helped ease near-term inflation concerns, supporting overall market sentiment.
Lower energy prices could improve the outlook for consumer-related sectors, airlines, logistics and selected manufacturing companies by reducing input and transportation costs.
"Domestically, the market should continue to find support from improving economic fundamentals. We expect the FBM KLCI to trade above the 1,700 range next week as investors balance improving geopolitical sentiment," he told Bernama.
For the week just ended, Bursa Malaysia extended its gains for a seventh consecutive session, supported by strong buying in selected blue-chip and heavyweight counters.
On a Friday-to-Friday basis, the key index rose 28.40 points to 1,712.03 from 1,683.63 a week earlier.
On the index board, the FBM Emas Index soared by 206.43 points to 12,681.05, the FBMT 100 Index strengthened 203.68 points to 12,519.62, the FBM Emas Shariah Index jumped 136.36 points to 12,558.66, while the FBM Mid 70 Index garnered 278.44 points to 18,162.58, and the FBM ACE Index leapt 123.85 points to 4,792.52.
By sector, the Plantation Index swelled 121.14 points to 8,873.22, the Energy Index declined 9.10 points to 766.27, the Financial Services Index surged 560.22 points to 20,204.58, and the Industrial Products and Services Index trimmed 6.36 points to 190.57.
Weekly turnover decreased to 16.92 billion units valued at RM14.62 billion from 17.32 billion units valued at RM13.74 billion a week earlier.
The Main Market volume expanded to 9.73 billion units valued at RM13.06 billion from 9.48 billion units valued at RM12.06 billion previously.
Warrants turnover slipped to 4.18 billion units valued at RM538.65 million compared with 5.28 billion units valued at RM815.97 million last week.
The ACE Market volume expanded to 2.99 billion units valued at RM1.01 billion from 2.55 billion units valued at RM854.07 million in the previous week.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: PM: Petronas' role expansion in Turkmenistan boosts Malaysia's global energy standing
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The success of Petroliam Nasional Bhd (Petronas) in expanding its role in Turkmenistan's energy sector opens access to some of the world's largest gas reserves, thus strengthening Malaysia's position in the global energy landscape, said Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim.
He said the development reflected high confidence in Malaysia's capabilities as well as Petronas' excellent track record of having been established and operating in Turkmenistan for three decades.
On Saturday, Petronas, through its wholly owned subsidiary Petronas Carigali (Turkmenistan) Sdn Bhd,
signed several new agreements
to strengthen long-term strategic cooperation with the country.
Among the new agreements are collaborations with State Concern Turkmennebit and State Enterprise Hazarnebit to strengthen its presence in the Caspian Sea and expand its upstream portfolio in the country.
Anwar said the two-day official visit, from June 18 to 19, to the Central Asian country also saw the signing of various cooperation documents involving education, diplomatic training, aviation, science and human capital development.
"I am confident that the agreements reached will elevate Malaysia-Turkmenistan relations to a stronger level for the prosperity of the people and the interests of the country," he said in a post on X today.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump, blaming alleged vandals, says Washington Reflecting Pool needs repairs
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump said on Saturday that much of the water at the newly renovated Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool "probably" has to be drained for repairs following what he called "vandalism" of the site.
"Many additional people have been arrested having to do with the disgraceful vandalism of our beautiful Reflecting Pool," Trump said on social media.
"We met with contractors today, will probably be forced to release and drain much of the water in order to do the necessary repairs, but will have them done as quickly as possible."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NGO proposes standardised education for better resettlement outcome
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The lack of a standardised education pathway for refugee children in Malaysia affects their long-term resettlement prospects and academic mobility, said a non-governmental organisation (NGO) that provides learning services.
The NGO, recognised by the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR), has supported nearly 800 students through learning centres nationwide.
It was established in 2010, although enrolment has declined in recent years due to financial constraints.
A spokesperson said the country's refugee education landscape remained fragmented, with learning centres having to adapt to changing policies on registration and examination access.
She said the absence of a consistent framework had made it difficult to ensure continuity in education, particularly for students later resettled to third countries.
The spokesperson said many refugee learning centres had registered as Alternative Learning Centres (ALCs) in 2019, but the registrations were cancelled following the Covid-19 pandemic, leaving providers without a clear pathway.
The uncertainty, she said, resulted in learning centres switching between the Malaysian syllabus and international systems such as the International General Certificate of Secondary Education (IGCSE), depending on available resources and policy direction.
Despite the challenges, she said Sijil Pelajaran Malaysia (SPM) remained the most practical option because it was more affordable and accessible.
She said refugee students who sat  the SPM as private candidates had gone on to be resettled in the United States, New Zealand and the Philippines, with some achieving straight As despite studying in non-formal settings.
"Even with limitations, our students are capable of succeeding," she said, adding that many were placed into education systems abroad through age-based assessments.
However, she said education and resettlement outcomes remained inconsistent due to differing third-country policies, scholarship opportunities and admission requirements.
While health authorities had been supportive, she said education remained an area without a clear framework.
She said Malaysia's refugee education system would benefit from better coordination between government agencies and learning providers.
"Health agencies are very supportive, but in education there is still no clear framework or visible engagement. At the moment, we are working without a proper legal framework or clear guidance," she said.
"We see ourselves as complementing the system, not replacing it.
"We are not just trying to educate them academically. We want to develop responsible people who can contribute to society," she added.
The spokesperson said misinformation and viral narratives surrounding isolated incidents have contributed to stigma and made community outreach more difficult, including public acceptance of refugee education initiatives.
She said one of the NGO's learning centres has halted operations recently to ensure the safety of students and teachers.
"Even if it were to reopen, parents are likely unwilling to allow their children to attend.
"Everyone stays at home with very limited movement outside the house. We may even have to consider closing the school entirely," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump says 'no tolls' in Strait of Hormuz for 60 days, warns of possible US fees later
Article:
ISTANBUL: US President Donald Trump said Saturday that no maritime fees will be permitted in the Strait of Hormuz during the current 60-day ceasefire, warning that Washington may impose its own tolls thereafter, Anadolu Ajansi reported.
After the 60 days, fees might be collected for "services rendered as the guardian angel" to the Middle East countries to ensure "reimbursement of costs" for the past and future, should a final deal not be completed, said Trump.
"There will be no tolls ... unless they are imposed by and for the United States of America," the US president wrote on his social media platform Truth Social.
The ultimatum follows an announcement from Iran's Khatam al-Anbiya Central Headquarters that the
waterway will be closed
due to alleged US breaches of commitment and Israeli ceasefire violations in Lebanon.
However, US Central Command (CENTCOM) dismissed the claim, asserting that US forces remain "present and vigilant" to ensure all aspects of the agreement are obeyed, adding that maritime traffic remains "intact".
"Iran does not control the Strait of Hormuz," CENTCOM spokesperson Captain Tim Hawkins told Axios. – Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: King, Queen extend Father's Day greetings
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: His Majesty Sultan Ibrahim, King of Malaysia, and Her Majesty Raja Zarith Sofiah, Queen of Malaysia, have extended Father's Day greetings to all fathers in the country.
"Fathers are the pillar of strength in a family and the guiding force shaping their children's future.
"Thank you for the sacrifices, love and invaluable guidance. Though rarely expressed in words, a father's contributions and love remain eternal in the heart," read a post on Sultan Ibrahim Sultan Iskandar Facebook page.
The King and Queen also offered prayers for their late fathers.
"May Allah bestow His blessings upon our late fathers, Almarhum Sultan Iskandar, and Almarhum Sultan Idris Shah II."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Resettlement isn't just a host country problem' - Experts
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:  Third-country resettlement of refugees must be treated as a shared international responsibility rather than one that falls disproportionately on transit or host countries, experts said.
Human Rights Commission of Malaysia (Suhakam) commissioner Datuk Ahmad Azam Ab Rahman said a sustainable approach requires stronger burden-sharing among the international community, alongside closer cooperation between governments, United Nations agencies and civil society organisations.
"Refugee protection is a shared international responsibility and should not be borne only by transit or host countries," he said.
Refugees awaiting resettlement, Azam said, should be allowed to live in dignity in transit countries, including access to regulated employment, healthcare and education.
He said this would reduce dependency, improve self-reliance and benefit local communities because access to such services can support long-term social stability and contribute positively to the economy.
Azam also called for stronger resettlement pathways, including education, employment and family reunification routes.
"Education-based pathways remain among the most effective options, supported by scholarships, language training and skills development," he said.
"In this regard, such an approach represents a 'win-win' arrangement, where refugees who are provided with access to education are better equipped to contribute to their communities."
On Malaysia's long-standing call to consider acceding to the 1951 Refugee Convention and its 1967 Protocol, Azam said it would clarify legal obligations and strengthen protection standards while balancing national interests and security concerns.
However, UKM International Law Associate Professor Dr Salawati Mat Basir said Malaysia's non-signatory status still carries legal and practical implications.
Salawati said ratification would significantly expand legal obligations and could increase regional pressures if not supported by broader international burden-sharing mechanisms.
The country, she said, remains bound by the principle of non-refoulement under customary international law, which prohibits returning individuals to places where they may face persecution or serious harm.
Salawati also cautioned against proposals to centralise refugees in a single large settlement, similar to Bangladesh's Cox's Bazar.
She said this model is neither viable nor sustainable in Malaysia's context, given the financial, security and administrative demands involved.
She added large-scale settlements would require housing, healthcare facilities, schools and security systems, all of which involved significant long-term costs and governance capacity.
"It is not just about placing people in one area. It requires full infrastructure and continuous administration," she said.
"Concentrating refugees in a single location could also create additional operational and security risks," she added.
Salawati said Malaysia should focus on strengthening existing mechanisms, including structured registration systems and coordinated data-sharing with the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees  (UNHCR) to improve oversight.
Meanwhile, Bernama quoted the UNHCR as saying that refugees must respect and comply with local laws as Malaysia  recognises the need to protect those fleeing conflict and persecution.
UNHCR representative in Malaysia Louise Aubin said refugees are not immune to the laws of a country .
"All refugees must abide by the law. Refugees are not immune to the laws of a country.
"So there's a mutual respect that is established. It's almost like a contract between the hosting state and refugees. Refugees must respect the law," she  said.
She said this on the sidelines of the Kuala Lumpur Conference: Solidarity with Refugees held on World Refugee Day here yesterday.
Addressing concerns about the Rohingya community, Aubin said the UNHCR applies stringent requirements when determining refugee status, and not everyone who comes to Malaysia qualifies as a refugee.
Aubin said UNHCR conducts extensive interviews and cross-checks information before recognising a person as a refugee.
"UNHCR can cross-check information with its other offices in and around the world to verify that people who say who they are and what they have fled is actually true," she added.
Aubin also welcomed Malaysia's efforts to implement the Refugee Registration Document Programme.
She said these efforts would help ensure refugees do not remain displaced indefinitely and may eventually return home when conditions permit.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Stepsister to testify as Zara Qairina inquest resumes
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: The late Zara Qairina Mahathir's stepsister is among the witnesses expected to testify when the inquest resumes tomorrow.
Lawyer Shahlan Jufri, who represents Zara Qairina's mother, Noraidah Lamat, said the girl is now an adult.
On Tuesday, handwriting expert Dr Linthini Gannetion is expected to testify before coroner Amir Shah Amir Hassan.
In the following days, the proceedings are expected to hear testimony from child psychologist Dr Aishah Rosli and psychiatrist Dr Simon Wong.
The inquest entered its 72nd day during the last proceedings last month, with Zara Qairina's maternal uncle, Abu Bakar, his wife Nur Shira Abdullah, and her cousin testifying.
Consultant forensic psychiatrist Dr Chua Sze Hung and Assistant Superintendent of Police Mohd Zaidi Abu Hassan were also recalled last month.
Assistant medical officer Mohd Faiszal Md Saimi, who was the first responder to examine the then 13-year-old after she was found unconscious on the ground floor of the SMKA Tun Datu Mustapha hostel on July 16 last year, was among the witnesses who testified.
Since the inquest began on Sep 3 last year, more than 70 witnesses have been called as of press time.
In previous proceedings, the Coroner's Court was also told that Zara Qairina's parents are expected to testify.
Zara was found unconscious on the ground floor of the SMKA Tun Datu Mustapha hostel on July 16 and was pronounced dead the following day at Queen Elizabeth Hospital.
The Anti-Bullying Act, which aims to address bullying cases involving minors, came into force on June 16 with the official launch of the Anti-Bullying Tribunal.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Weak quake hits Batu Pahat waters early morning
Article:
BATU PAHAT: A weak earthquake measuring 3.3 magnitude was recorded in the waters off Batu Pahat early this morning.
The Malaysian Meteorological Department (MetMalaysia) said in a statement that the quake occurred about 20km southwest of Batu Pahat.
It said the earthquake was centred at coordinates 1.7 degrees north and 102.7 degrees east, at a depth of 10km.
"The weak earthquake was recorded at 1.17am and tremors may be felt around Johor. MetMalaysia will continue to monitor the situation," it said in a Facebook post.
It added that the department would continue monitoring the situation from time to time.
MetMalaysia also urged residents who felt the tremors to fill in a survey form via the following link: https://forms.gle/CyUwHQE9LnyuWaJ37
The quake this morning was the fifth recorded in the district, following the last occurrence on March 14 in the waters off Batu Pahat, which measured 3.2 magnitude.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Rafizi: Bersama to announce Johor polls candidates on Friday
Article:
KOTA BARU: Parti Bersama Malaysia (Bersama) will announce its line-up of candidates for the Johor state election in Johor Baru on Friday.
Party leader Datuk Seri Rafizi Ramli said the screening process for prospective candidates had been completed, although he did not disclose the number of seats the party intended to contest.
He said the party had received an encouraging response after opening applications slightly more than a week ago to those interested in contesting in the Johor and Negri Sembilan state elections.
"We have already received applications from more than 300 individuals.
"All applicants are required to complete the necessary forms and undergo an interview process, including answering random questions based on their personal background and experience," he said when speaking at the Jelajah Kancil programme at Pantai Cahaya Bulan here last night.
The Election Commission has fixed July 11 as
polling day
for the Johor state election, with nomination day set for June 27 and early voting on July 7. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Big' refugee registration plan earns broad support [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: As the world marked Refugee Day yesterday, the government's plan to implement the Refugee Registration Document Programme (DPP) has been warmly received by various groups.
The groups believe it may lead to a win-win situation where the rights of refugees are protected as they do their part in contributing to the  economy.
On June 15, Home Minister Datuk Seri Saifuddin Nasution Ismail described refugee management as a long-standing "legacy issue" that requires a structured, fact-based approach to resolve.
Saifuddin said the government is working on a framework to balance security and humanitarian concerns, besides addressing gaps in official data.
He said the implementation of the DPP under the Immigration Department is part of efforts under National Security Council Directive No. 23 (Revised 2023) to improve documentation and oversight.
SAFEGUARDS, TRANSPARENCY
The Human Rights Commission of Malaysia (Suhakam) described the DPP as a "promising and necessary step" towards structured refugee governance.
But it said the programme must be implemented transparently with strong safeguards.
Commissioner Datuk Ahmad Azam Ab Rahman said Malaysia's policy gap has created uncertainty, exploitation risks and inconsistent treatment of refugee communities.
A more structured framework,  Azam said, could improve coordination and documentation, reduce risks of arbitrary detention and exploitation, and expand access to basic services and lawful livelihoods.
He said currently, there is little information about the implementation details, including verification mechanisms and safeguards.
Gaps in clarity, he said, may expose refugees to scams and fraudulent registration schemes.
"There is now limited information on how the programme will be implemented, including safeguards and verification features.
"Greater transparency is important to avoid confusion and misinformation at the grassroots level," he said.
Azam added that a strong engagement between refugees, civil society organisations, employers and enforcement agencies is needed to ensure accurate information is disseminated, noting that scams have been reported following the announcement of the DPP's implementation.
"We are particularly concerned that the absence of clear and accessible information may create opportunities for people to exploit vulnerable refugee communities through false promises or counterfeit documents," he said.
Azam noted that refugee governance must not be viewed solely through immigration control or labour needs, but anchored in legal safeguards and human rights protections.
"Any long-term refugee management framework should include clear legal safeguards, protection against refoulement, discrimination, trafficking and arbitrary detention, as well as access to healthcare, education and livelihood opportunities," he said.
[[nid:1468565]]
He added that Malaysia's approach should balance orderly management with dignity, protection and social inclusion for refugee communities.
EMPLOYMENT PROSPECTS
The Malaysian Employers Federation (MEF) also supports the introduction of the DPP, but it is concerned about the implications on hiring refugees to work.
MEF adviser Datuk Shamsuddin Bardan said Malaysia's growing labour shortage highlights the need to better regulate access to an already present but informal refugee workforce.
Shamsuddin said around 80 per cent of refugees and asylum-seekers are of employable age, pointing to a potential labour pool to fill shortages in the construction, agriculture and manufacturing sectors.
Many refugees, he said, are already participating in the economy without legal recognition or protection.
He said they are currently not permitted to work, pushing many into informal arrangements that expose them to exploitation.
"When they are in the informal sector, there are no formal contracts.
"Allowing them to work formally would require employers to provide proper protection through an employer-employee relationship," he said.
However, Shamsuddin said employers are concerned about cost implications if the system becomes similar to foreign worker requirements, including the payment of  levies, Employees Provident Fund, Social Security Organisation, medical screening and accommodation.
He said clear guidelines are needed on permits, obligations and implementation, adding that past efforts had mixed results due to job mismatches and weak coordination.
He added that employers are prepared to play their role, but a proper framework is essential.
"A clear framework will provide certainty for employers, ensure protection under labour laws and allow better regulation of workforce participation, without imposing the full cost structure applied to newly recruited foreign workers," he said.
According to the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR), Malaysia is hosting about 215,600 refugees and asylum-seekers up to the end of February.
Of these, 193,824 are from Myanmar, including 126,144 Rohingyas, 15,774 Chins and 33,002 others from conflict-affected or persecuted communities.
The remaining 21,776 are from more than 50 countries, including Pakistan, Yemen, Somalia, Afghanistan, Syria, Sri Lanka, Palestine and Iraq.
UNHCR data also shows that 46 per cent of refugees and asylum seekers are men and 24 per cent are women, while 64,680 are children  aged below 18.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Experts push for stronger safety net for elderly care
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia must build a comprehensive, integrated safety net for its ageing population to ensure sustainable and dignified elderly care, experts said.
Malaysian Coalition on Ageing chairman Cheah Tuck Wing said support should be extended to children caring for elderly parents.
"Some parents experience neglect by their children, often stemming from financial and care-giving constraints, particularly among the 'sandwiched' generation.
"Cases of dementia, cancer, stroke and other non-communicable diseases are rising, requiring specialised care and stronger government support," he said.
He said support should include cash aid, tax relief and subsidies for housing renovations to create age-friendly facilities and help prevent injuries.
Cheah also urged stronger community involvement in supporting elderly people living alone.
"Time-banking is a good way to encourage voluntarism while ensuring that elderly people are well taken care of, in addition to the various initiatives to support this group.
"Ageing care should be a whole-of-nation approach, that involves children, community, civil societies, the private sector and the government."
Time-banking is a system where individuals accumulate volunteering hours that can later be exchanged for care.
[[nid:1468569]]
Malaysian Research Institute on Ageing director Associate Professor Dr Rahimah Ibrahim said families were struggling to meet care-giving demands as public healthcare resources remain stretched.
She said proposals for long-term care (LTC) insurance, inspired by Singapore's CareShield Life, must be part of a wider framework.
"However, any discussion on LTC insurance cannot happen in isolation. It must be part of a broader framework that includes national health insurance and dependent coverage," she said.
She said Singapore's model integrated healthcare and insurance schemes to support citizens across life stages.
"Malaysia lacks such a comprehensive ecosystem. While we have the Employees Provident Fund (EPF) and Social Security Organisation (Perkeso), these focus mainly on retirement savings and workplace injuries."
She added that gaps between healthcare and welfare systems, financial constraints among the B40 group and cultural expectations of family care-giving remained key challenges.
"A comprehensive long-term care insurance system is not just about caring for the elderly. It is about protecting families, supporting women — who are often primary caregivers — and ensuring social and economic stability.
"By creating an integrated safety net, we can reduce the burden on families, empower caregivers and build a society that values dignity at every stage of life," she said.
Rahimah also proposed a one-stop platform for health, LTC and dependent insurance using existing infrastructure such as Perkeso and EPF.
She said the Older Persons Bill should drive broader reforms in supporting Malaysia's ageing population, including measures to promote inclusivity across age, gender and employment, such as encouraging re-employment of older people with targeted incentives.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Former Egyptian doctor faces trial after hospital abuse claims
Article:
CAIRO: A former Egyptian doctor is to stand trial after prosecutors on Saturday accused her of spreading false information through a social media post alleging abuses against women at a major university hospital in Alexandria.
Omnia Swaydan was arrested at her home in Egypt's Nile Delta province of Beheira last week and questioned on charges of spreading false news and misusing social media, according to her lawyers, Asmaa Naeim and Mohamed Ramadan.
She was later released on bail of 20,000 Egyptian pounds (US$401), they said on social media.
Swaydan's Facebook post detailed allegations of obstetric violence, patient neglect and degrading treatment of women at Alexandria's Al-Shatby University Hospital, where she trained as a medical resident between 2020 and 2021.
Egypt's public prosecution said on Saturday that it had referred her to criminal court over the post.
It added that Swaydan told investigators some incidents were based on procedures she had witnessed during her training but that, due to her "limited experience", she believed they were unusual and outside accepted medical practice.
Prosecutors said she also acknowledged that some claims had been relayed to her "by others whom she was unable to identify" and that she had not verified whether they had occurred.
In the now-deleted post, Swaydan alleged widespread abuse at the hospital, including claims that a doctor sexually violated a patient during labour and later mocked the incident, and that another woman was slapped and verbally humiliated while giving birth.
She also described the alleged denial of care to vulnerable patients, including a sexual assault survivor who was dismissed and refused treatment, and a critically ill pregnant woman who she said was denied emergency intervention without proof of marriage.
Before the prosecution's announcement, Alexandria University said it would investigate the allegations, adding that any abuses would be addressed "with full firmness and without cover-up or exceptions".
The Doctors' Syndicate said it had not received any formal complaints regarding the claims and urged anyone with evidence to submit it through official channels.
Following Swaydan's post, several women on social media reported similar experiences in public hospitals across Egypt, including Al-Shatby.
AFP was unable to independently verify Swaydan's claims or those shared by other women.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: French police raid house for drugs and find a Picasso
Article:
CRETEIL, France: A police raid looking for drugs at a house in a Paris suburb turned up a painting by Pablo Picasso, prosecutors said Saturday.
"This discovery was made during a search carried out as part of an investigation into drug trafficking," said the public prosecutor's office of Creteil, south-east of Paris.
They had opened an investigation into theft and dealing in stolen goods, the statement added.
On Friday, four people were brought before a court for an immediate hearing in connection with the case, the office said.
According to newspaper Le Parisien, which first reported the story, the search was carried out on Monday by investigators in Champigny-sur-Marne, a town east of Paris.
As well as the Picasso, police seized cannabis resin, luxury clothing and several thousand euros in cash, the newspaper said.
The Creteil prosecutor's office said the painting had been authenticated as a work by the Spanish painter, but did not specify which painting it was.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Decoding a father's love language
Article:
MY FATHER never hugged me. At least, not that I can remember.
He never told me he loved me, never sat beside me when my heart was broken, or whispered words of comfort when life disappointed me.
My memories of him were filled instead with stern looks, impossible standards, and one recurring message: "If you did wrong, live with it, learn from it. Don't complain. Move on."
As a boy, I hated hearing those words. As a young man, I vowed never to become him.
As an adult, I thought I had succeeded. Until one quiet afternoon.
I was flipping through an old, dusty photo album when I stopped at a picture of my late father taken when he was about 60.
I stared — the eyes. The stern expression hiding more than it revealed. The stubbornness etched into his face. The look of a man who had carried too much for too long. And suddenly, at 60  myself, I realised something that broke my heart.  I had spent most of my life trying not to become him… and somehow, I had become him anyway.
Memories I had buried slowly returned — not as accusations, but as questions. Was he distant? Or merely exhausted? Did he not love? Or did he love in ways a child was never meant to understand?
It took me more than half a century to realise that some fathers are not good at saying, "I love you". Instead, they spend their entire lives proving it — quietly, relentlessly and often unnoticed.
I was the ninth child in a family of 10. The first five siblings had only one girl — my late eldest sister. The last five had only one boy — Me!
That sounds special until you realise it was mostly awkward. My closest brother in age was more than 10 years older, and by the time I was old enough to understand the world, my brothers had left home for university, work, or simply life.
My mother passed away in 1970 when I was only 3. I have no memory of her voice, her touch or her holding me when I cried.
But I remember my father. Not for hugs or comforting words, but for his firmness. If I failed, I was expected to learn from it. If I cried, I was expected to recover. If life was unfair, well, life had never promised otherwise. At the time, I thought he was cold.
Growing up, I was secretly envious of my older siblings. They told stories of a different father — a gentler father. A father who laughed more. A father who teased them, took them out and occasionally let mischief slide.
I listened with disbelief. Who was this man? Because the father I knew was all discipline, expectations and impossible standards. Then I realised something. My siblings grew up with two parents. I grew up with a man trying to be two.
After my mother's passing,  my father changed. Not overnight. But slowly. The warmth became responsibility. The laughter became sacrifice. His focus shifted to one mission: his children must succeed — not just survive. Succeed!
Education was sacred. Discipline was non-negotiable. Failure was terrifying, not because it disappointed him, but because he feared life would crush us if we were unprepared.
My brothers had freedom. They climbed trees, got into trouble and came home laughing. I, on the other hand, had rules. Many rules.
As the only boy left at home, I was monitored like a national security project. There was no freedom, no excuses, no shortcuts… I felt suffocated.
Whenever I worked with him, I felt inadequate. Nothing I did seemed right — the way I spoke, the way I held tools, the way I approached life. Everything about him clashed with everything I thought I was.
So, I made a vow. I would never become my father. I would be softer, warmer, more expressive.  And then in the 1980s, life, with its wicked sense of humour, sent me to the United Kingdom.
Years later, in the early 1990s, my father visited me in Oxford with my sisters. He was already in his late 70s. Age had caught up with him. The man who once seemed larger than life now moved more slowly. He tired easily. When my sisters went shopping or sightseeing, he often stayed behind.
And for the first time in my life, it was just the two of us. No rules. No expectations. No lectures. Just silence. And conversations. Wonderful conversations.
We talked about books, history, politics, life.  He was witty, terribly sarcastic, sharp as a razor. His English was impeccable. He lived to read.
And for the first time in my life, I stopped seeing my father. I saw a widower. I saw a man who had buried the love of his life at 50 and carried his grief in silence for more than 20 years. I saw loneliness disguised as discipline. I saw sacrifice mistaken for coldness.
And I hated myself a little for taking so long to see him. I later discovered why he never remarried. He feared a stepmother might not love the younger children as her own. So, he sacrificed companionship… for us.
My father never spent money on himself, not because he was stingy, but because every ringgit he earned had already been spent in his mind — on his children's education, their books, their futures and dreams he would never live long enough to see himself.
And I realised something else. All those years I thought he never loved... Perhaps he simply loved in a language I never understood.
His love was not hugs. It was sacrifice. It was waking up every day after losing the love of his life and choosing not to collapse.  It was denying himself comfort so his children could have opportunities. It was standing firm when life demanded softness because softness, he feared, might not save us.
When I returned from the UK, I stayed with him for a while. I helped take care of him before beginning my own life. And during those final months, I finally understood — why he was hard on me, why he demanded so much, why he never let himself fall apart.
I had mistaken strength for indifference, discipline for lack of love, silence for absence of emotion. I was wrong. My father cared deeply. He just wasn't very good at saying it.
He passed away in 1994 at the age of 74. And strangely, I did not lose him then. I keep finding him — in my stubbornness, in my perfectionism, in the way I shoulder responsibility, in how I hide my worries from those I love.
Sometimes I smile at the irony. The man I spent years trying to escape became the man I miss the most. I look like him. I think like him. I carry burdens the way he did.
And perhaps, I love the way he loved too — awkwardly, quietly, imperfectly, but with everything I have. Maybe that is why fathers and sons often struggle; they are too alike, too stubborn to admit love, too proud to apologise, too busy trying not to become each other. Only to realise, years later, that the greatest parts of ourselves came from them all along.
This Father's Day, if your father is still around, call him. Even if the relationship is awkward. Even if he never hugged you. Even if he never said, "I love you".
Some fathers were raised in a generation that taught them to swallow their fears, hide their tears and prove their love through sacrifice.
And sometimes, the deepest love is not the one we hear. It is the one we spend a lifetime finally learning to recognise. Mine never said, "I love you".
But now, after all these years… I know he did.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Al Jazeera says Israeli strike kills journalist in Gaza
Article:
DOHA: Qatar-based Al Jazeera said on Saturday that an Israeli strike killed one of its journalists in the Gaza Strip, the latest of several people working for the broadcaster killed in the Palestinian territory since Oct 7, 2023.
An Israeli military spokesman told AFP the military "confirms it carried out a strike on Ahmed Wishah, who was a Hamas terrorist", but did not provide any evidence to support the accusation.
"Ahmed Wishah, a cameraman for Al Jazeera, has been killed in an Israeli airstrike that targeted a house in the Bureij refugee camp in central Gaza," Al Jazeera said on its website.
A correspondent for the channel said the strike was carried out by a drone in the refugee camp and also wounded several people.
The broadcaster said Wishah's brother and fellow Al Jazeera journalist Mohammed had been killed in April "by Israeli shelling when he was travelling in his vehicle."
The Israeli military said in a statement issued late Saturday that Ahmed Wishah was killed in a "precise strike" alongside two other Hamas fighters and that he had served as a "sniper operative" in Hamas.
"Alongside his work as an Al Jazeera photojournalist in recent years, Wishah was an operative in Hamas' military wing," the military said.
"In recent months, he advanced sniper attack plans and additional terrorist activities against IDF troops operating in the Gaza Strip," the military said, adding that he had worked alongside his now killed brother Mohammed.
"Due to his recent military activity and the threat he posed to IDF troops operating in the area, he was eliminated in a precise aerial strike," the military statement said. It provided no evidence in support of its claim that Ahmed Wishah was a Hamas fighter.
When Mohammed Wishah was killed in April, media rights group Reporters Without Borders (RSF) said Israeli forces had killed more than 220 journalists since the war in Gaza erupted. At least 70 of them were killed in the context of their professional duties.
The war in Gaza started after Hamas's deadly assault on Israel on Oct 7, 2023.
The Israeli army has repeatedly said it never deliberately targets journalists.
But since October 2023, it has claimed to have killed a number of people who it says were Palestinian militant "terrorists" working under the guise of being media professionals.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Stepping up for the father who walked her home [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: For Salinah Adelinah Abdullah, giving up her dream of joining the Royal Malaysia Air Force was only the first of many sacrifices that would define her life.
After her adoptive mother died in January 2024, she left her job in Melaka to care for her adoptive father, Ahmad Mohammed Tahir, 70, whose health had deteriorated following a stroke and neurological complications.
"My father was in critical condition. He frequently suffered seizures and was placed in a medically induced coma three times.
"The last time he was induced, doctors asked me to gather our family members. I vowed that if my father recovered, I would resign from my job to care for him.
"When he regained consciousness, I kept my promise. To me, my father's sacrifices in raising me mean more than anything else," she told the
New Straits Times
.
Salinah said her father was born with a physical disability that left one of his legs shorter than the other. But his condition never stopped him from doing everything he could for her.
"From age 5, my father walked me to school every day. After dropping me off, he would wait for me at a small hut near a village grocery shop.
"On rainy days, the old zinc roof leaked and soaked his clothes. On hot days, he would be drenched in sweat.
"Yet he would still be there waiting for me. We would then walk the 7km home. With every uneven step he took, he held my hand tightly.
"If it rained, he would pull out a plastic sheet to cover my head.
"He sacrificed every ounce of his strength so that I could live comfortably like everyone else."
She said one of her fondest childhood memories was her father's determination to buy her a bicycle despite the family's financial difficulties.
"My father and I would walk around the neighbourhood.
"He would hold my hand with one arm and carry a heavy bucket of ice with the other. The money he earned selling ice was saved entirely for me — to buy my bicycle and pay for my school trips.
"Every cent went towards my future, never for himself."
Ahmad is now bedridden and frequently suffers swelling in his legs, a condition that worsened after a major surgery to remove his gallbladder and part of his damaged intestines.
Despite the demands of full-time caregiving, Salinah refuses to see it as a burden.
[[nid:1468493]]
To earn a living, she now hosts live broadcasts on social media to sell homemade sambal, often with her father by her side.
"Every day begins before dawn. After preparing breakfast, I clean and care for my father.
"From 10am to noon, I become his 'workout coach' and help with his rehabilitation.
"I also host live broadcasts on TikTok to sell  sambal,
nasi impit daun pisang
, sambal
penyet
and sambal
paru
.
"The word 'tired' does not exist in my vocabulary because my greatest commitment is to continue caring for my beloved father."
While many see her dedication as a way of repaying her parents, Salinah views it differently.
"People often tell me I am repaying them for their kindness, but what they do not know is that my debt to my mother and father can never truly be repaid.
"I can never match the strength and goodness they poured into my life. If God were to measure my father's uneven steps against what I have done, I would lose."
She said although her father and late mother were her adoptive parents, they had always been her true mother and father.
"The world may describe them as my adoptive parents, but to me, they are my real mother and father.
"Only I know the depth of their love and how hard they worked to raise me.
"I quit my job and sell sambal to bring comfort to my father. He fulfilled my every wish when he was able to.
"Now it is my turn to fulfil his and give back the happiness he gave me."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Driver killed, 28 in hospital as UK train collision probed
Article:
BEDFORD: UK accident investigators were on Saturday probing the cause of a train collision north of London which killed a driver and injured scores of passengers, nine critically.
The crash Friday afternoon near Bedford, a town around 90 kilometres (55 miles) north of the British capital, involved two London-bound trains on the same track, according to East Midlands Railway (EMR), which operates both services.
Transport Secretary Heidi Alexander said it was "too early to speculate" on the cause of the crash while vowing "a thorough investigation ... to ensure that lessons are learnt".
British Transport Police Chief Constable Lucy D'Orsi said in an update Saturday near the scene that more than 80 people had been treated for injuries in hospital and 28 remained there.
"Nine are in a critical condition," she added.
Police had earlier confirmed that the driver of one of the trains had died at the scene.
D'Orsi said that "specialist investigators from British Transport Police are working with colleagues at the Rail Accident Investigation Branch (RAIB) to gather the facts and determine what has happened".
'SUDDENLY' HIT
Buckingham Palace issued a statement saying King Charles was "greatly saddened" by the crash and sent "his thoughts and sympathies" to the dead man's family and to those injured.
A passenger on one of the trains, Paul Cavin, told the BBC: "We had stopped and suddenly we were hit from behind pretty bad."
"There were people injured on my carriage," he said, adding he could see many wounded people walking away from the trains, some with "smashed up noses".
The East of England Ambulance Service said Saturday 11 people sustained "very serious" injuries, while a further 32 suffered serious wounds and 56 others had minor injuries.
It dispatched more than 20 ambulances, specialist hazardous area rescue teams, along with six air ambulances.
The area's fire and rescue service said it mobilised more than 20 fire and specialist vehicles, and at the height of the response, more than 70 firefighters and officers were involved at the scene.
[[nid:1468521]]
Another passenger, Brett Byatt, told BBC radio it felt "surreal" the morning after and that he had "moved into the stage of anger now".
"I don't know at whom," he added. "But it's more about (the fact) we've got one of the oldest railway networks and signal failures happen a lot... Why wasn't that signalled to my train?"
Officials have not said whether signalling issues played a role in the collision.
'PROFOUNDLY SAD'
EMR's managing director Will Rogers called it "a profoundly sad day for the railway community".
"We are deeply saddened that our driver has tragically died, and a number of other people have suffered injuries," he said, speaking at the scene alongside other officials.
He noted the train operator was "fully supporting" the RAIB probe.
Train collisions are relatively rare in the United Kingdom.
In September 2023, several people were injured after two trains collided at Aviemore station in the Scottish Highlands.
The crash happened on the Strathspey Railway, a heritage railway run separately from mass-transit public services, and involved a stationary carriage and another train in the station.
In August 2020, an early morning service from Aberdeen to Glasgow came off the tracks, killing three people and injuring six near the town of Stonehaven, northeast Scotland, after a landslip caused by heavy rain.
Network Rail – an arm of the UK Department for Transport – pleaded guilty in 2023 to safety failings at a court hearing and the public body was fined £6.7 million (US$8.4 million).
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Retaining older employees crucial as population ages, says MEF
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:   Malaysia's ageing population is expected to shrink the workforce and weigh on economic growth, prompting calls for measures to keep older people in employment, an employers' group said.
Malaysia became an ageing nation in 2020, with more than 7.5 per cent of its population aged 60 and above.
By 2044, 14 per cent of the population will be over 60, rising to more than 20 per cent by 2056, when Malaysia is projected to become a super-aged nation.
Malaysian Employers Federation (MEF) president Datuk Dr Syed Hussain Syed Husman said the demographic shift presents challenges for productivity and economic growth, requiring coordinated efforts from the government, employers, workers and trade unions.
He said Malaysia's fertility rate had fallen from 6.7 children per woman in 1957 to 1.6 in 2022, limiting future workforce growth.
"As the population ages, the working-age group will shrink, potentially slowing economic growth," he said.
Syed Hussain said retaining older workers would be crucial, noting that many wished to remain active and financially independent despite health-related challenges.
A 2023 MEF survey found that 31.6 per cent of companies had policies to hire workers above 60, while 59.1 per cent did not.
He urged the government to allocate sufficient resources and develop comprehensive policies to address ageing-related challenges.
"Engagement with stakeholders is essential to ensure effective solutions," he said.
MEF also encourages employers to rehire retirees or offer fixed-term contracts to retain expertise, reduce recruitment costs and ensure continuity.
Labour shortages are also driving  adoption of automation and advanced technologies, including robotics and artificial intelligence, particularly in manufacturing, agriculture and logistics.
"Upskilling and reskilling initiatives, including investments in technical and vocational education and training, are critical to prepare workers for changing industry demands," he said.
He added that employers should promote age-friendly workplaces, mentorship programmes and inclusive policies.
"Older workers value flexibility and opportunities to share expertise, while younger employees seek guidance.
"Mentorship programmes and digital skills training can support both groups and foster a collaborative workplace."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Albanian protests against Trump-linked resort swell
Article:
TIRANA: Thousands of protesters marched through Albania's capital Saturday, one of the largest demonstrations since rallies against a planned resort linked to US President Donald Trump's family began almost three weeks ago.
Since late May, protesters have gathered every evening to oppose the construction of a luxury hotel linked to Trump's daughter Ivanka and her husband Jared Kushner, planned in a nature reserve on the Balkan nation's coast.
Opposition to the development has become a flashpoint for frustrations over perceived corruption, with demands now including the resignation of Prime Minister Edi Rama.
Although official numbers were not available, AFP journalists said the crowd appeared to be the biggest since demonstrations began.
Boosted by a large number of Albanian diaspora who travelled to the country for the rally, a sea of marchers waved Albanian and American flags as red balloons were released into the air.
The protesters said they would stay in the streets until Rama resigns. Some protesters projected the slogan "Albania is not for sale" on the PM's office building.
The protesters accuse him and his government of a lack of transparency concerning the developments planned on the Albanian coast.
"We are not against the country's development; we are against the arrogance and lack of transparency surrounding projects that affect our lives," said one protester, Alma, a student at the Faculty of Science.
[[nid:1468470]]
RAMA DEFIANT
Rama, who has remained defiant throughout the protests, told a party meeting earlier Saturday that he would not resign. He claimed that Kushner's involvement, rather than concerns about the development itself, was fuelling the protests.
"The world did not wake up from the fate of Narta, but from the name of Kushner and the shadow of Trump," he said.
The wave of demonstrations was sparked after barbed-wire fencing and bulldozers appeared on a quiet beach in the coastal area of Zvernec in the protected Vjosa-Narta area – about 100 kilometres (60 miles) southwest of the capital, Tirana.
Clashes between local protesters and private security at the proposed hotel site sparked outrage and drew large crowds to marches within days.
The Kushner-backed resort, along with another development on nearby Sazan Island, are both planned near a key breeding site for migratory birds – including hundreds of flamingos.
"The Vjosa-Narta landscape, including the Zvernec region, is one of Albania's most valuable natural ecosystems, and its importance extends far beyond the country's borders," Olsi Nika, a biologist and aquatic ecologist, said at the protest.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Report says UK PM Starmer ready to quit, but source says he is still focused on the job
Article:
LONDON: Britain's Observer newspaper said Prime Minister Keir Starmer was expected to resign on Monday and set out a timetable for his departure, though a government source said Starmer remained focused on getting on with the job of governing.
The threat to Starmer's position, which has been building for months, increased sharply on Friday when his rival Andy Burnham won a seat in Parliament that would allow him to launch a formal leadership challenge.
The Observer report said Starmer was discussing the matter with his wife at his Chequers country residence before making a final decision, but that senior Labour figures expected a clear statement on his future as early as Monday.
However, a government source said Starmer remained focused on his job and pointed to previous statements he has made to that effect.
The British leader said on Friday he would fight any challenge to his leadership and urged Labour not to tear itself apart with infighting.
STARMER'S POPULARITY HAS PLUMMETED
Starmer led the centre-left Labour Party to a landslide election win in 2024 but has become deeply unpopular after a series of scandals and policy U-turns that have given many voters an overall impression that he cannot deliver the improvement to their standards of living that he promised.
If he were to quit or be ousted, it would mean the country installing its seventh prime minister in just over a decade - the highest turnover in nearly two centuries, reflective of anger at successive governments' failures to improve public services and tackle issues like illegal immigration.
More than 100 elected lawmakers in Starmer's party - roughly a quarter of all Labour representatives in the House of Commons - have publicly said they want him to quit or set out a timetable for his exit, according to a Reuters tally.
The Observer report, which did not name its sources, said Starmer had reached the conclusion that his position was no longer tenable after speaking to cabinet ministers, advisers, donors and trade union leaders.
BURNHAM WAITING IN THE WINGS
Burnham, a 56-year-old career politician, is seen by many in Labour as the most likely successor to Starmer - whether through a negotiated transfer of power or a formal leadership contest.
Having built a power base within Labour as mayor of Greater Manchester in northern England, he comfortably saw off the threat from Nigel Farage's right-wing populist party to win an election for a vacant parliamentary seat on Friday.
Burnham did not immediately make a formal challenge to Starmer but used his victory address to promise a new path for the country. His allies have urged Starmer to agree to step down and hand over power voluntarily.
Former health minister Wes Streeting has also said he is willing to challenge Starmer.
The Times newspaper reported on Saturday that Burnham would sack Finance Minister Rachel Reeves if he were to become prime minister after his advisers concluded she did not represent a sufficient change of direction. Reuters could not immediately verify that report.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cut red tape to ease seniors' woes, says care home manager
Article:
PUCHONG:   Juriah Abdul Hamid, 87, appears in good spirits as she joins other elderly residents singing the late Sudirman's
Hore Hore
at Rumah Jagaan dan Rawatan Orang Tua Al Ikhlas.
"How are you? I love to sing; it makes me happy," she says as she greets centre manager Mohd Arif Abdullah Azman.
Juriah is among 40 senior citizens living at the home here, which has a staff of 18.
Arif said Juriah's children were unable to care for her and chose to place her at the facility.
"It's tough for the children too.
"Many of them aren't able to care for their elderly parents financially, physically and emotionally," he told the
New Straits Times
during a visit.
He welcomed the  Older Persons Bill, saying it could streng-then protection for the elderly.
He also called for action to address bureaucratic hurdles affecting access to public assistance.
"Many of these people aren't able to move freely due to health and mobility issues, yet they are expected to be present at the respective government agencies monthly to verify their applications for assistance.
"There are many challenges in operating a licensed home, mostly because of bureaucracy, despite the availability of
wakaf
land, courtesy of generous Malaysians."
The logistics graduate said he had continued the
work started by his mother, a nurse at a government healthcare facility.
He said the family first took in an elderly patient who had been neglected at the hospital where she worked.
"From then on, we started operating the centre from a flat unit before moving to a proper home on
wakaf
land in Puchong.
"We mostly accept those who are bedridden and have no one to look after them.
"We have had to turn away many others who are still mobile and have children due to space constraints."
Arif said the proposed law should also strengthen financial protection for the elderly, noting that many fall victim to scams and lose their life savings.
"My hope is, of course, for every senior citizen to be cared for by their loved ones.
"Meanwhile, those who are unfortunate should not be left behind and should be able to spend the rest of their lives with dignity."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US and Iran set for new talks after delay and deadly strikes
Article:
GENEVA: A new round of negotiations over the Middle East war was set to kick off Sunday, with Iranian negotiators arriving in the Swiss host city hours ahead of US Vice President JD Vance, even as Teheran said it was closing the Strait of Hormuz again over Israeli attacks in Lebanon.
Before boarding his flight to Europe, Vance told reporters he hoped to "make progress on the nuclear issue, make progress on the Lebanon ceasefire issue. Those are the two big things that I think we're going to be focused on."
Follow-up talks had been planned in Switzerland on Friday but were postponed at the last minute after Israel launched deadly strikes in Lebanon following the deaths of four of its soldiers in combat.
Washington announced a renewed ceasefire there later Friday – a condition of its preliminary agreement with Iran – but Israeli troops clashed again with Hizbollah fighters on Saturday, with each side accusing the other of breaking the truce.
Citing a US "breach of contract" and "the Zionist regime's continuous and relentless violation of the ceasefire in southern Lebanon", Iran's central military command said "the Strait of Hormuz will be closed to vessel traffic".
Hormuz, a key conduit for oil and gas shipments, was blockaded by Iran for much of the war, sending shockwaves through global energy markets.
Teheran had agreed to reopen it under the preliminary accord signed by President Donald Trump and his Iranian counterpart Masoud Pezeshkian, and shipping traffic had begun to recover.
US Central Command said after Iran's announcement that safe passage through the international waterway had "remained intact" and that US forces were "present and vigilant".
Trump later warned that Washington could impose its own tolls on Hormuz if negotiators failed to complete the deal.
There would be no tolls "unless they are imposed by and for the United States of America", Trump wrote on Truth Social.
[[nid:1468464]]
SWITZERLAND PUSH
An Iranian delegation arrived in Switzerland late Saturday, state media and the Swiss foreign ministry said.
Iran's official broadcaster said it included parliamentary speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf and Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi.
Foreign ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baqaei said the delegation would "demand implementation of the other party's commitments" under the deal.
"Otherwise, the entire understanding will be in trouble," he said, according to official news agency IRNA.
Vance left Washington on an afternoon flight to join the talks, saying he could only stay "a day or two".
US negotiators Jared Kushner and Steve Witkoff were already there handling "some of the technical elements" and had reported that "things are going well," Vance said in an interview with Fox News earlier Saturday.
Mediator Pakistan – whose interior minister was reportedly in Iran on Saturday for meetings with officials – said "technical-level talks" were scheduled for Sunday in Burgenstock, Switzerland, with Pakistani and Qatari mediators joining US and Iranian representatives.
The talks are meant to open a two-month negotiation period on issues left unresolved by the initial accord, notably Iran's nuclear programme.
[[nid:1468463]]
LEBANON TRUCE FRAYS
Israel and Hizbollah continued trading accusations Saturday as fighting persisted in southern Lebanon.
The Israeli military said one soldier was killed in combat, the fifth such fatality since the US-Iran deal was reached.
An Israeli army official later said the military had received orders from the country's political leadership to cease fire, adding that troops were "not conducting proactive strikes" but operating defensively inside a security zone.
Earlier, an Israeli military official said fresh attacks were under way after Hizbollah "launched more than 50 projectiles at Israeli forces in southern Lebanon" overnight.
Hizbollah accused Israel of carrying out "under the cover of the ceasefire...an infiltration attempt towards the Ali Taher hills", a strategic feature overlooking Nabatieh, and said its fighters had responded "with appropriate weapons".
Lebanese state media reported Israeli air raids on around 20 locations, with authorities counting more than 30 dead. The overall death toll from the fighting in Lebanon had surpassed 4,000, the health ministry said.
Hizbollah lawmaker Hassan Fadlallah said his group retained "the full right to confront this enemy when it attacks us".
Israel's US ambassador Yechiel Leiter maintained it was Hizbollah that broke the truce, saying Israel was "defending itself against terrorist attacks".
But Hizbollah said Israel bore "full responsibility".
Fadi Zayat, who fled the southern Lebanon town of Tayr Debba, told AFP that "fear dominates" the south.
"We returned to the village a few days ago, but our bags are ready to flee again," the 53-year-old said.
Hizbollah pulled Lebanon into the wider Middle East conflict in early March when it fired rockets at Israel in retaliation for the killing of Iran's supreme leader in US-Israeli strikes.
A previous ceasefire meant to take effect in Lebanon in April was never honoured, with
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Older Persons Bill counts on changing mindsets
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The proposed Older Persons Bill aims to prioritise family reconciliation while setting out a legal framework to support government initiatives on elderly care.
Women, Family and Community Development Minister Datuk Seri Nancy Shukri said the framework was a key component under consideration following engagements with ministries, agencies, state governments, non-governmental organisations and civil society groups.
Malaysia is at a critical juncture in its demographic shift towards an ageing population.
The Statistics Department projects those aged 65 and above will rise from 8.1 per cent in 2024 to 14.5 per cent by 2040, in a population of about 34 million.
Experts say this will place increasing pressure on the care system.
Nancy said the framework was  intended to define and align the roles and jurisdictions of federal and state authorities on elderly welfare, with details of discussions remaining confidential pending cabinet review.
"We want to ensure that Malaysia is fully prepared for the infrastructure and systemic demands of an ageing nation," she told the
New Straits Times
.
"It is important to look at our national strategy through two parallel tracks, namely developmental incentives and legal protections."
She said measures such as tax rebates, housing benefits and welfare support for co-living families were outlined in the Senior Citizens Action Plan 2026-2030.
Currently, the government provides RM600 in monthly assistance to eligible older persons through the Social Welfare Department to help them remain with their families or within their communities.
Recipients must be Malaysian citizens aged 60 and above, with household income below RM1,236 and registered with the department.
Additionally, the Bantuan
Pesakit Terlantar scheme provides RM500 a month for families caring for bedridden or chronically ill patients, subject to financial need.
Nancy said the bill was designed to complement these measures by providing a comprehensive legal framework.
"The bill is not designed to be purely punitive. The ministry's philosophy relies heavily on education, community awareness and shifting societal mindsets.
However, awareness alone cannot protect the most vulnerable.
"The proposed act is designed as a comprehensive framework that pairs fair enforcement with robust public advocacy, ultimately aimed at strengthening family harmony and community support for elderly well-being," she said, adding that feedback from experts and NGOs had been welcomed.
She said the objective was
to strike a balance between enforcement and advocacy, with the bill providing a legal mechanism to address elder abandonment and neglect.
"It goes beyond penalties. It establishes a framework to safeguard older persons from physical, emotional and financial abuse, ensuring they can age with dignity, respect and security."
Previously reported as the Senior Citizens Bill, the proposed legislation is expected to be tabled in the Dewan Rakyat in the fourth quarter of this year.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bolivia declares state of emergency, deploys military to quell protests
Article:
LA PAZ: Bolivia's president on Saturday declared a state of emergency and deployed soldiers and bulldozers to raze anti-government roadblocks that have paralysed the Andean nation.
For more than six weeks, unions, Indigenous groups and coca farmers have marched through cities and blocked roads across the country with rubble, logs and debris in protest of the conservative government.
Major cities have suffered acute shortages of fuel, food and medicine, the economy has lost billions of dollars and the protests have threatened to topple Bolivia's first non-socialist government in two decades.
President Rodrigo Paz on Saturday appeared in a predawn televised address to warn protesters they would face "the full force of the law" as he moved to end the crisis.
He declared a 90-day state of emergency, which curbs the right to protest and allows the military to be deployed domestically.
Hours after his address, AFP reporters in the city of El Alto saw squads of soldiers and armed police moving in a convoy as bulldozers moved in to clear roadblocks.
Some residents clapped as they passed. One man handed a bag of bread to a police officer riding in the back of a pickup truck.
"I'm very happy" Carla Butron, a 39-year-old shopkeeper, told AFP. "everything has been difficult here in El Alto during these 50-some-days – work, free movement."
In nearby La Paz, military police and navy personnel guarded the presidential palace and police tactical units were stationed on main squares.
"Bolivians cannot continue to be held hostage by blockades that prevent them from working, studying, receiving medical care, getting supplies and bringing food to their homes," Paz said in a social media post.
"This state of emergency is not intended to take away normalcy, but to restore it."
The protesters want Paz to abandon liberal economic reforms and step down, less than a year after he was elected.
The 58-year-old had signalled he was ready to negotiate and, earlier this week, agreed to a deal with one of the country's major unions to end the crisis.
In exchange for a promise not to privatise state companies and to hold further talks, the Bolivian Workers' Central union agreed to end their protests.
But some Indigenous groups have vowed to fight on, and more than 40 major roadblocks remain.
"We want him gone. We don't want him to be the one governing," Lidia Callisaya, a 42-year-old Aymara leader, told AFP recently.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: King, Queen honour fathers’ sacrifices in Father’s Day message
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, June 21 — His Majesty Sultan Ibrahim, King of Malaysia, and Her Majesty Raja Zarith Sofiah today extended Father’s Day greetings to all fathers.
In a post on Sultan Ibrahim Sultan Iskandar’s official Facebook page, His Majesty said fathers are a source of strength within the family and play a vital role in shaping the future of their children.
Sultan Ibrahim also expressed gratitude for the sacrifices, love and guidance of fathers, describing them as invaluable.
Although rarely expressed in words, His Majesty said a father’s love and sacrifices remain forever cherished in the hearts of his children.
“May Allah Subhanahu Wa Ta’ala bestow His blessings upon my late father, Almarhum Sultan Iskandar, and Almarhum Sultan Idris Shah II,” His Majesty said. — Bernama

Title: Petronas’ expanded role in Turkmenistan boosts Malaysia’s global energy position, says Anwar
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, June 21 — The success of Petroliam Nasional Bhd (Petronas) in expanding its role in Turkmenistan’s energy sector opens access to some of the world’s largest gas reserves, thus strengthening Malaysia’s position in the global energy landscape, said Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim.
He said in a post on X today that the development reflected high confidence in Malaysia’s capabilities as well as Petronas’ excellent track record of having been established and operating in Turkmenistan for three decades.
On Saturday, Petronas, through its wholly owned subsidiary Petronas Carigali (Turkmenistan) Sdn Bhd, signed several new agreements to strengthen long-term strategic cooperation with the country.
Among the new agreements are collaborations with State Concern Turkmennebit and State Enterprise Hazarnebit to strengthen its presence in the Caspian Sea and expand its upstream portfolio in the country.
Anwar said the two-day official visit, from June 18 to 19, to the Central Asian country also saw the signing of various cooperation documents involving education, diplomatic training, aviation, science and human capital development.
“I am confident that the agreements reached will elevate Malaysia-Turkmenistan relations to a stronger level for the prosperity of the people and the interests of the country,” he said. — Bernama

Title: Dzulkefly: 402 vape cases involving synthetic drugs give strong basis for national vape ban
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, June 21 — Data on vape seizures by the Royal Malaysia Police (PDRM) up to April this year recorded 402 cases involving vape devices and liquids found to have been mixed with various types of dangerous synthetic drugs.
Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad said the evidence provides a strong basis for the government to move towards banning the use of vapes in the country.
“The detection of synthetic drugs such as benzodiazepine, nimetazepam, MDMA, cannabinoids, tetrahydrocannabinol and methamphetamine in vape preparations is clearly prohibited and illegal for use, particularly among minors or persons below the age of majority.
“On the strength of that evidence alone, there is already a strong basis pointing towards a vape ban. That is a compelling argument. The matter is currently under the government’s consideration, and the Ministry of Health (MOH) is also involved in the process,” he said here today.
He said this at a press conference after launching the Cik Era Rides the MRT Programme at the Tun Razak Exchange (TRX) MRT Station.
On June 11, Deputy Inspector-General of Police Tan Sri Ayob Khan Mydin Pitchay said the emergence of a new synthetic drug known as “Piu Piu”, which has been detected in electronic cigarette liquids, demonstrated why the devices should be banned.
Elaborating further, Dzulkefly said the MOH views seriously the proliferation of vape liquids found to contain dangerous synthetic drugs and stressed the government’s commitment to addressing the threat through a comprehensive approach.
He said enforcement efforts are no longer borne solely by the MOH but are being carried out aggressively through strategic cross-agency cooperation, including with the Ministry of Home Affairs (KDN) and the PDRM.
Earlier, Dzulkefly launched the Cik Era Rides the MRT Programme, an initiative aimed at promoting public health in a relaxed and interactive manner to approximately 200,000 daily passengers on the MRT Putrajaya Line, from Putrajaya Sentral MRT Station to the TRX MRT Station.
The programme is a continuation of the Journey Home with Cik Era campaign held in March to introduce Cik Era AI, an artificial intelligence-based virtual companion application that provides digital guidance for individuals seeking to quit smoking or vaping.
Since its launch on March 15, Cik Era AI has recorded 17,412 user interactions, averaging 258 interactions daily. The latest promotional initiative, implemented through a strategic collaboration under the mQuit Services Memorandum of Understanding (MoU), has increased the daily interaction rate by 34 per cent to 347 interactions per day as of June 15.
In addition to leveraging AI technology, the MOH has strengthened access to nicotine addiction treatment through the JomQuit platform, which brings together 90 registered private service providers and has assisted 9,349 clients since October 2024.
Support initiatives such as mQuit, JomQuit and Cik Era AI complement the enforcement of the Control of Smoking Products for Public Health Act 2024 (Act 852) in efforts to reduce the burden of chronic diseases and realise the aspiration of a smoke-free generation in Malaysia. — Bernama

Title: Dzulkefly: MOH fast‑tracking study on e‑MC system to curb medical certificate forgery and identity theft
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, June 21 — The Ministry of Health (MOH) is studying the implementation of a digital medical certificate (e-MC) as part of efforts to curb abuse, forgery and identity theft involving medical practitioners by syndicates dealing in fake sick leave certificates.
Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad said the ministry’s Digital Health Division has been instructed to expedite a study to facilitate the transition to a more secure digital platform for medical certificates.
“Medical certificates (MCs) can only be issued by a doctor or medical officer treating the patient. That is clear. This ethical misconduct is a serious issue, and the MOH will not compromise on it under any circumstances.
“Perhaps this is also a clear signal for us to move towards a digital system or e-MC. This is the way forward in minimising abuse,” he told a press conference after launching the ‘Cik Era Naik MRT’ programme at the Tun Razak Exchange (TRX) MRT station here yesterday.
He was commenting on a case involving five individuals, including a nurse in Pekan, Pahang, who have been remanded to assist in investigations into the sale and purchase of suspected fake MCs, as well as the existence of the ‘holiday master’ website syndicate, which has been actively forging the names of doctors and private clinics since 2016.
Regarding the ‘holiday master’ syndicate, which was found to have stolen the professional registration numbers of private medical practitioners for profit, Dzulkefly stressed that the case is currently under investigation by the Malaysian Medical Council.
“The Malaysian Medical Council will be the lead agency in this matter and will work closely with law enforcement authorities. The MOH will also examine the implications from the perspective of internal data breaches to ensure that our doctors’ identities are not misused indiscriminately,” he said.
In a separate development, Dzulkefly cautioned the public against using artificial intelligence (AI) as a tool for self-diagnosis, particularly for high-risk chronic diseases such as cancer and heart conditions.
